one_line_takeaway: RAVEN is a four-agent (Explorer/Analyst/Reporter/Judge) RAG pipeline that turns vulnerable code into Google-Project-Zero-style vulnerability reports, but on 105 NIST-SARD memory-bug samples with open-weight Falcon models it tops out around 7.5/10 judged quality and a 54.21% remediation success rate, with no RAG configuration winning consistently across models.
key_finding: Across the 4x10 grid (Table II), overall report-quality scores (Soverall, 0-10 scale) ranged from 7.34-7.68 for Falcon H1-34B-Instruct (highest-performing model, mean ~7.52), 6.73-7.32 for Falcon H1R-7B (mean ~7.07, and highest variance across configurations), 6.50-6.90 for Falcon H1-7B-Instruct (mean ~6.74), down to 5.72-6.11 for Falcon3-10B-Instruct (mean ~5.89); the paper explicitly states no single RAG configuration outperforms the others across all models. Deep-diving the highest-variance model (Falcon H1R-7B), the best Factual Grounding configuration was Contextual Chunking + Hybrid Retrieval + LLM Reranker (6.14), followed by Contextual Chunking + Embeddings + LLM Reranker (6.02) and Flat Chunking + Hybrid Retrieval + Cross-Encoder Reranker (5.98); the same two configurations led on Code Reasoning Quality (7.10 and 7.32 respectively) and Remediation Quality (6.98 and 6.80). Structural Integrity losses were attributed to formatting defects (corrupted code rendering, truncated fixes, literal '\n' escapes) and false negatives (misclassifying vulnerable code as non-vulnerable, which auto-zeros the score). Averaged over the best configuration per model, RAVEN's agentic workflow achieved a 54.21% remediation success rate, where success requires both fix_addresses_root_cause and syntax_valid to hold per both judges; qualitatively, Falcon H1R-7B tended to produce syntactically valid fixes that suppress/bypass the bug rather than fix its root cause, while Falcon H1-34B-Instruct produced syntactically valid code but degraded on fixes requiring intent or multi-step control-flow reasoning.
core_contribution: RAVEN, a four-agent LLM pipeline (Explorer, Analyst, Reporter, Judge) that, given a vulnerable source-code file, retrieves context from a purpose-built RAG knowledge base (70 scraped Google Project Zero 0-day root-cause-analysis reports plus the 1,321-entry MITRE CWE catalog) and synthesizes a structured, Google-Project-Zero-style vulnerability report (vulnerability analysis, exploit analysis, fix generation). The paper's own contribution also includes a task-specific LLM-as-a-Judge protocol (two judge LLMs, Claude 4.5 Sonnet and Gemini 3.1 Pro, scoring Structural Integrity, Ground-Truth Alignment/Factual Grounding, Code Reasoning Quality, and Remediation Quality) and an empirical sweep of 4 open-weight Falcon models across 10 RAG configurations (3 chunking strategies x mix of 3 retrieval methods x 2 rerankers) on 105 NIST-SARD memory-vulnerability samples.
threat_model: Not applicable in the adversarial sense — RAVEN is a defensive/analyst-assistance tool for documenting already-identified vulnerable code, not an attack or exploitation system, and the paper does not study attackers, adversarial inputs, or system compromise. The only adversarial-robustness angle mentioned (LLM-judges' robustness to adversarial manipulation) appears solely in the Related Work discussion of a cited paper [29] and is not evaluated by RAVEN itself.
limitations: Evaluation relies entirely on LLM-as-a-Judge scoring (no human expert validation of generated reports) and a binary syntax_valid flag rather than actual compilation/execution/test-suite verification of generated patches. Only Falcon-family open-weight models (4 variants) were evaluated for the Explorer/Analyst/Reporter roles; no comparison to other LLM families (e.g., GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama) that are commonly used elsewhere in the literature, despite those models being used as judges. Evaluation set is limited to 105 synthetic NIST-SARD samples spanning 15 CWE types, all memory-corruption-focused and skewed toward CWE-119 (31.43% of the set); results may not generalize to real-world CVEs or non-memory-safety vulnerability classes, and there is a domain mismatch between the synthetic SARD eval set and the real-world Google Project Zero RCA corpus used for retrieval. The paper explicitly reports that no RAG configuration dominates consistently across models, undermining any single 'best pipeline' recommendation. No ablation isolates the individual contribution of the Explorer, Analyst, or Reporter agent stages, nor does the paper report latency/compute cost for the 4-agent pipeline or the OCR-based data collection pipeline. Best achieved overall quality tops out at ~7.68/10 (Soverall) and 54.21% remediation success rate, indicating the system is far from reliably producing production-grade root-cause-analysis reports without human review.

datasets_used: NIST-SARD (Software Assurance Reference Dataset, SAMATE project; ~450,000 test cases across 150+ weakness classes overall) — evaluation subset of 105 memory-focused vulnerable code samples spanning 15 CWE types used to benchmark RAVEN
Google Project Zero '0-days-in-the-wild' RCA reports (70 reports after filtering 89 crawled URLs) — ingested as RAG knowledge base content
MITRE CWE catalog v4.17 (2,735-page PDF, 1,321 CWE entries) — OCR'd and ingested as RAG knowledge base content
quantitative_results: Overall Soverall by model (best-to-worst RAG configuration range, Table II): Falcon H1-34B-Instruct 7.34-7.68 (mean ~7.52); Falcon H1R-7B 6.73-7.32 (mean ~7.07); Falcon H1-7B-Instruct 6.50-6.90 (mean ~6.74); Falcon3-10B-Instruct 5.72-6.11 (mean ~5.89)
Falcon H1R-7B Factual Grounding (0-10) by best configs: Contextual+Hybrid+LLM Reranker = 6.14 (best); Contextual+Embeddings+LLM Reranker = 6.02; Flat+Hybrid+Cross-Encoder = 5.98
Falcon H1R-7B Code Reasoning Quality: Flat+Hybrid+Cross-Encoder = 7.32 (best); Contextual+Hybrid+LLM Reranker = 7.10
Falcon H1R-7B Remediation Quality: Contextual+Hybrid+LLM Reranker = 6.98 (best); Flat+Hybrid+Cross-Encoder = 6.80
Average CWE remediation success rate across chosen best configurations per model: 54.21% (success = fix_addresses_root_cause AND syntax_valid, agreed by both judges)

Test-set CWE composition (Table I): CWE-119 = 31.43%; CWE-787, CWE-476, CWE-457, CWE-416, CWE-170, CWE-122, CWE-121, CWE-120 each = 6.67%; CWE-415 and CWE-401 each = 5.71%; CWE-193, CWE-126, CWE-124, CWE-123 each = 0.95%

Experimental scale: 4 Falcon models x 10 RAG configurations = 40 experiments, each over 105 samples, with 2 independent LLM judges scoring 4 dimensions (8 criterion scores averaged per sample into Soverall)
baselines_compared: No external baseline systems (e.g., prior automated vulnerability-report generators or non-agentic LLM prompting) are compared; the comparative structure is entirely internal: 4 Falcon model variants (Falcon-H1R-7B, Falcon-H1-7B-Instruct, Falcon-H1-34B-Instruct, Falcon3-10B-Instruct) evaluated across a 10-way RAG configuration grid (chunking x retrieval x reranker combinations) serving as the paper's own ablation/comparison baseline
